title: European History
intro: European History questions concentrate heavily on national and regional overviews, with Hungary, Portugal, Switzerland, France, Greece, Sweden, Italy, Poland, and Romania among the single most-asked answerlines, alongside major cities like Constantinople, Florence, Venice, Prague, and Vienna. Medieval and early modern material forms a dense core: Charlemagne and the Carolingians, the Byzantine Empire, the Crusades, the Hundred Years' War, and the Thirty Years' War recur alongside towering monarchs such as Charles V, Louis XIV, Catherine the Great, and Peter the Great, whose reigns anchor discussion of dynastic rivalry, absolutism, and state-building from the Habsburgs, Ottomans, and Romanovs to the Vasas and Hohenzollerns.

Modern political history supplies a second major cluster. The French Revolution and Napoleon Bonaparte give way to the nationalist movements that unified Italy and Germany and unraveled the Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian empires, while the long arc of British and Irish history runs from the Glorious Revolution through Chartism, Irish Home Rule, and the Troubles. The twentieth century is dominated by the two World Wars and the totalitarian movements that framed them, Stalin, Hitler, and Mussolini foremost among them, followed by the Cold War's division of the continent, its 1989 revolutions, and the later wars of Yugoslav dissolution.

A substantial share of answerlines fall outside strict chronology: dynasties and royal houses (Habsburg, Bourbon, Plantagenet, Romanov) recur across centuries, as do generic regnal names shared by many rulers, and a long tail of lower-frequency answerlines covering economic and social history, guilds, trade goods like wool, silk, and wine, universities, feudal institutions, and everyday practices such as witch trials and dueling, that surface across many individual topics rather than anchoring their own.

sections.name: Antiquity, Late Antiquity, and Byzantium
sections.blurb: Classical Greece and Rome, Rome's transformation into a Christian empire, and the Byzantine Empire down to the fall of Constantinople in 1453.

entries.note: Byzantine capital founded by Constantine, besieged repeatedly before falling to the Ottomans under Mehmed II in 1453.
Mediterranean island of Minoan civilization, later Venetian and Ottoman territory, united with Greece in 1913.
Greek-speaking Eastern Roman Empire ruled from Constantinople, codified by Justinian, that fell to the Ottomans in 1453.
Byzantine emperor who codified Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis and reconquered Italy and North Africa via Belisarius.
Italian capital of the ancient Republic and Empire, later the papal seat and unified Italy's capital from 1871.
Ostrogothic king who ruled Italy from Ravenna after overthrowing Odoacer, blending Roman and Gothic rule.
Ancient civilization on the Nile, later a Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman province before modern independence.
Egyptian city founded by Alexander the Great, famed for its ancient library and later a major Byzantine see.
Byzantine religious controversy over the destruction of religious images, spanning the 8th and 9th centuries.
Roman emperor who ended the Crisis of the Third Century via the Tetrarchy and persecuted Christians.
Caucasian kingdom converted to Christianity in late antiquity, later absorbed into the Russian and then Soviet empire.
Western Roman and later Ostrogothic and Byzantine capital in Italy, famed for its early Christian mosaics.
Nomadic Hunnic ruler who invaded the Roman Empire, checked at the Catalaunian Plains, dying in 453.
Roman emperor who legalized Christianity, won at the Milvian Bridge, and founded Constantinople as his new capital.
Macedonian king who conquered the Persian Empire and pushed to India, spreading Greek culture widely.
Byzantine general under Justinian I who reconquered North Africa and Italy from Vandals and Ostrogoths.
Macedonian king who unified Greece after Chaeronea, father of Alexander the Great.
Byzantine historian under Justinian I, author of both official histories and the scandalous Secret History.
Elite Byzantine imperial bodyguard composed largely of Norse and later English warriors.
Byzantine emperor whose appeal for help against the Turks helped trigger the First Crusade.
Byzantine princess and historian whose Alexiad chronicles her father Alexios I's reign and the First Crusade.
Direct-democracy Greek city-state led by Pericles, center of classical culture defeated by Sparta.
Athenian reformer credited with founding democracy by reorganizing citizens into ten tribes.
Germanic people split into Visigoths and Ostrogoths, who defeated Rome at Adrianople and later sacked it.
Ancient and Byzantine Hellenic people whose modern nation won independence from the Ottomans in the 1820s.
Byzantine imperial dynasty that instituted Iconoclasm, beginning with Leo III.
Byzantine emperor who codified Roman law and reconquered Italy and North Africa via Belisarius.
Byzantine emperor who repelled an Arab siege of Constantinople and launched Iconoclasm.
Judean fortress where Jewish rebels made a mass-suicide last stand against besieging Roman forces.
532 Constantinople chariot-racing riot that nearly toppled Justinian I before being brutally suppressed.
Levantine maritime traders from Tyre and Sidon who spread the alphabet and founded Carthage.
Boeotian Greek city-state that broke Spartan hegemony at Leuctra under Epaminondas.
Influential Byzantine empress and wife of Justinian I, credited with steadying his rule during the Nika riots.
Major Byzantine port city in Greece, second only to Constantinople in importance.
Roman engineering works channeling water across long distances via gravity, still standing across Europe.
Rome's first emperor, born Octavian, who defeated Mark Antony and Cleopatra to found the Principate.
Byzantine emperor called "Bulgar-Slayer" for crushing the First Bulgarian Empire, expanding the empire's power.
Roman orator, consul, and philosopher who exposed the Catiline conspiracy and was killed in the Second Triumvirate's proscriptions.
Wealthy Peloponnesian trade city whose quarrel with Corcyra helped trigger the Peloponnesian War.
Byzantine emperor who defeated Sassanid Persia but soon lost the Levant and Egypt to Arab conquest.
325 CE church council convened by Constantine that produced the Nicene Creed against Arianism.
Roman general and dictator who conquered Gaul, crossed the Rubicon, and was assassinated on the Ides of March.
Roman general who reformed the legions to accept landless citizens and fought Sulla in Rome's first civil war.
Incendiary naval weapon used by the Byzantines to devastating effect against enemy fleets.
Roman emperor who built a wall across northern Britain and consolidated the empire's frontiers.
Enslaved serf population of Messenia and Laconia bound to Spartan-owned land, periodically rising in revolt.
Byzantine emperor who defeated Sassanid Persia but soon lost the Levant and Egypt to Arab conquest.
"Father of History" whose Histories chronicle the Persian Wars and describe Egypt, Persia, and Scythia.
Foreign dynasty of likely Semitic origin that ruled Lower Egypt during its Second Intermediate Period.
Emperor who tried to revive traditional paganism against Christianity before dying campaigning against Persia.
Byzantine imperial dynasty founded by Alexios I, presiding over the empire during the Crusades era.
Spartan king who led the last stand of 300 at Thermopylae against Xerxes I's Persian invasion.
Byzantine eunuch general under Justinian I who completed the reconquest of Italy from the Ostrogoths.
Eastern Gothic people who ruled Italy under Theodoric the Great before Byzantine reconquest.
479 BCE battle in which the combined Greek army decisively defeated the Persians, ending Xerxes I's invasion.
Roman city buried by Vesuvius's 79 CE eruption, preserved and later excavated.
Roman general and triumvir who lost to Caesar at Pharsalus and was murdered in Egypt.
Byzantine historian under Justinian I, author of both official histories and the scandalous Secret History.
Early Christian theologian from Carthage, an influential early Latin church father.
Apostle traditionally considered the first bishop of Rome, foundation of papal authority.
Byzantine military-administrative provinces, developed to organize defense against Arab and other invasions.
Major Byzantine port city in Greece, second only to Constantinople in importance.
Phoenician port city famed for purple dye, besieged and captured by Alexander the Great.
Germanic people who crossed into North Africa, sacked Rome in 455 CE, and were later destroyed by Belisarius's reconquest.
Gallic chieftain who united tribes against Julius Caesar before his defeat at the siege of Alesia.

sections.name: Early Medieval Europe
sections.blurb: Germanic successor kingdoms, the Frankish Merovingian and Carolingian dynasties, Anglo-Saxon England, the Viking Age, and the earliest Slavic and Rus' states, roughly 400-1000.

entries.note: Frankish king crowned Holy Roman Emperor by the pope in 800, uniting much of Western Europe under Carolingian rule.
North Atlantic island settled by Norse Vikings, home to the medieval Althing assembly and the sagas.
Anglo-Saxon king of Wessex who repelled Viking invasions and promoted learning, later called "the Great."
Germanic people who sacked Rome in 410 and founded a kingdom in Iberia later conquered by the Umayyads.
Capital of Kievan Rus', the medieval East Slavic state from which Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus trace their origins.
Turkic-origin people who settled the Balkans and merged with Slavs to found the medieval First Bulgarian Empire.
First Frankish royal dynasty, founded by Clovis I, ruling Gaul until supplanted by the Carolingians in 751.
Scandinavian kingdom, Viking homeland later united with Denmark and Sweden before independence in 1905.
Germanic people who settled Anglo-Saxon England and were conquered and Christianized on the continent by Charlemagne.
Anglo-Saxon kingdom of the English Midlands, dominant under King Offa before absorption into Wessex-led England.
Frankish royal line founded by Pepin the Short, reaching its height under Charlemagne before fragmenting after 843.
First king to unite the Franks, converted to Catholic Christianity, founding the Merovingian dynasty.
Scandinavian kingdom, Viking homeland later united with Denmark and Sweden before independence in 1905.
Northern French duchy granted to Viking leader Rollo, launching point for William the Conqueror's 1066 invasion of England.
Saxon king crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 962 after defeating the Magyars at the Battle of Lechfeld.
Scandinavian seafaring raiders and traders who settled Normandy, England, and Kievan Rus' from the 8th to 11th centuries.
Turkic steppe khaganate north of the Black Sea whose elite reportedly converted to Judaism; later crushed by Kievan Rus'.
Germanic people who invaded Italy in the 6th century, founding a kingdom later conquered by Charlemagne's Franks.
English scholar who led Charlemagne's Carolingian Renaissance at the Palace School of Aachen.
Capital of Kievan Rus', the medieval East Slavic state from which Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus trace their origins.
Nomadic steppe people who settled the Carpathian Basin and founded Hungary after raiding Europe.
Medieval Russian trading republic and Hanseatic partner, ruled by a veche assembly before Ivan III's conquest.
Frankish leader who halted Umayyad expansion at the Battle of Tours in 732, grandfather of Charlemagne.
Norwegian king killed invading England at Stamford Bridge in 1066, days before the Norman Conquest.
Charlemagne's favored capital, site of his palace chapel and later the coronation city of German kings.
1066 battle in which William the Conqueror defeated Harold Godwinson, beginning Norman rule of England.
Comprehensive land survey commissioned by William the Conqueror in 1086 to assess England's wealth and taxation.
English monastic island whose 793 Viking raid is often considered the start of the Viking Age.
Viking-descended people of Normandy who conquered England in 1066 and Sicily in the 11th century.
Saxon king crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 962 after defeating the Magyars at Lechfeld.
Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Alfred the Great, whose dynasty eventually unified England.
Likely William the Conqueror, Duke of Normandy who defeated Harold at Hastings and became King of England.
Norman duke who defeated Harold Godwinson at Hastings in 1066, commissioning the Domesday Book.
Anglo-Saxon king whose poorly advised reign saw renewed Viking invasions and the Danegeld tribute payments.
Germanic people who conquered Gaul under Clovis and built the Carolingian Empire under Charlemagne.
Coalition of Viking forces that invaded Anglo-Saxon England in 865, conquering all kingdoms but Alfred's Wessex.
Arctic island settled by Norse Vikings under Erik the Red, later a Danish territory.
Medieval East Slavic federation centered on Kyiv, from which Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus trace their origins.
Powerful early Anglo-Saxon kingdom, a center of Christian learning at monasteries like Lindisfarne and Jarrow.
Likely Pepin the Short, first Carolingian king of the Franks and father of Charlemagne.
Anglo-Saxon monk whose Ecclesiastical History of the English People is a key source for early medieval England.
French city near which Charles Martel halted Umayyad expansion into Europe in 732.
Former Anglo-Saxon capital of Wessex under Alfred the Great, later an important Norman administrative center.
Last Anglo-Saxon king of England, killed at the Battle of Hastings by William the Conqueror in 1066.
Medieval Russian trading republic and Hanseatic partner, ruled by a veche assembly before Ivan III's conquest.
Powerful Anglo-Saxon king of Mercia who built the defensive earthwork Offa's Dyke against the Welsh.
First Carolingian king of the Franks, father of Charlemagne, who deposed the last Merovingian king.
Viking leader granted Normandy by the French crown, ancestor of William the Conqueror.
Legendary Varangian founder of the Rurikid dynasty that ruled Russia until the Romanovs.
Indo-European peoples who spread across Eastern Europe in the early Middle Ages, dividing into East, West, and South branches.
664 English church council that adopted Roman rather than Celtic Christian practices.
732 battle in which Charles Martel halted Umayyad expansion into Francia.
Danish king who ruled England, Denmark, and Norway in a North Sea Empire.
Danish king who ruled England, Denmark, and Norway in a North Sea Empire.
Region of England under Viking legal control after the Great Heathen Army's conquests.
Pious Anglo-Saxon king whose death without an heir triggered the succession crisis leading to 1066.
Norman-born queen consort of both Aethelred the Unready and later Cnut the Great of England.
Germanic seafaring people of the North Sea coast, converted to Christianity by missionaries like Boniface.
Danish king who united Denmark and Christianized it, commemorated by the Jelling stones.
William the Conqueror's brutal 1069-1070 campaign devastating northern England to suppress rebellion.
Saxon duke elected king of Germany, founder of the Ottonian dynasty, father of Otto the Great.
Visigothic Spanish bishop whose Etymologiae encyclopedia preserved classical knowledge into the Middle Ages.
One of the earliest Anglo-Saxon kingdoms, first to receive Christian missionaries under Augustine of Canterbury.
Western Frankish kingdom under the Merovingians, rival of Austrasia before Carolingian unification.
Nomadic steppe people who dominated the Carpathian Basin before Charlemagne crushed their khaganate.
Anglo-Saxon ship burial site in England, yielding a treasure trove revealing early medieval royal culture.
Kievan Rus' grand prince who codified law, built Kyiv's Hagia Sophia, and expanded dynastic marriage ties.
991 battle in which Vikings defeated Anglo-Saxon defenders, commemorated in an Old English poem.
1066 battle in which Harold Godwinson defeated Norwegian invader Harald Hardrada days before Hastings.
Embroidered cloth depicting the Norman Conquest of England, commissioned soon after the 1066 events.
Anglo-Saxon monk whose Ecclesiastical History of the English People is a key source for early medieval England.
Fortified Anglo-Saxon settlements built by Alfred the Great's dynasty to defend against Viking raids.
Frankish royal dynasty founded by Pepin the Short, reaching its height under Charlemagne.
Carolingian king who received West Francia at the Treaty of Verdun, later crowned emperor.
Alphabet developed from Greek script for Slavic languages, attributed to followers of Saints Cyril and Methodius.
Scandinavian people whose Viking raids and Danelaw settlement shaped early medieval England.
Weak later Merovingian kings whose power was overshadowed by their Carolingian mayors of the palace.
Pope who sent missionaries to convert Anglo-Saxon England and strengthened papal authority in the West.
Frankish bishop and historian whose History of the Franks is a key source for Merovingian-era Gaul.
Scottish island monastery founded by St. Columba, a key center of Celtic Christian missionary activity.
First Christian kingdom in Iberia, founded after the legendary Battle of Covadonga, origin of the Reconquista.
Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Alfred the Great, whose dynasty eventually unified England.
Carolingian emperor whose portion at the Treaty of Verdun became the contested "Middle Kingdom."
Frankish court office that grew to eclipse the Merovingian kings, held by Charles Martel and Pepin the Short.
First Frankish royal dynasty, founded by Clovis I, ruling Gaul until supplanted by the Carolingians in 751.
842 mutual pledges by Charlemagne's grandsons, an early written document in Old French and Old High German.
Powerful Anglo-Saxon king of Mercia who built the defensive earthwork Offa's Dyke against the Welsh.
Scottish archipelago long under Norse rule as a Viking earldom before passing to Scotland in 1472.
Ancient people of northern and eastern Scotland, eventually merging with the Scots to form the Kingdom of Alba.
Medieval East Slavic historical narrative tracing Kievan Rus' origins to the Varangian Rurik.
Medieval Russian trading city and republic closely tied to Novgorod before Muscovite annexation.
Enslaved people within Viking Age Scandinavian society, the lowest class in the Norse social order.
Medieval Russian principality and rival of Moscow for leadership among the Rus' before Muscovite dominance.
Islamic caliphate that conquered most of Iberia before Charles Martel halted its advance at Tours.
Ancient Swedish religious and later university center, seat of an archbishopric since the 12th century.
North American coastal region reached by Norse Vikings under Leif Erikson around the year 1000.
Bohemian duke and Christian martyr later canonized, celebrated in the carol "Good King Wenceslas."
Saxon leader who resisted Charlemagne's conquest and forced Christianization before eventually submitting.

sections.name: High and Late Medieval Europe
sections.blurb: The Crusades, the Holy Roman Empire and papacy, the Hundred Years' War and Wars of the Roses, the Iberian Reconquista, the Mongols, the Black Death, and medieval Central and Eastern Europe, roughly 1000-1500.

entries.note: Central European kingdom founded under Stephen I and the Arpads, later ruled by Ottomans, Habsburgs, and the Soviet bloc.
Central European kingdom of the Piast and Jagiellonian dynasties, later partitioned by Russia, Prussia, and Austria.
Mediterranean island kingdom ruled successively by Normans, Hohenstaufens, Angevins, and Aragonese; site of the Sicilian Vespers revolt.
Wealthy Valois duchy under dukes like Philip the Good and Charles the Bold, absorbed by France and the Habsburgs.
Bohemian capital under Charles IV, site of the defenestrations that sparked the Hussite Wars and the Thirty Years' War.
Southern German duchy, later kingdom, long ruled by the Wittelsbach dynasty from its capital Munich.
English king who conquered Wales, warred with Scotland's Wallace and Bruce, and issued key statutes and expulsions.
Mediterranean island held by the Knights Hospitaller, who withstood the Ottoman Great Siege of 1565.
Celtic principality conquered by Edward I of England after resistance from Llywelyn and later Owain Glyndwr.
1337-1453 series of conflicts between England and France over the French throne, featuring Joan of Arc and Agincourt.
1397-1523 personal union joining Denmark, Norway, and Sweden, ended by Swedish secession under Gustav Vasa.
1135-1153 English civil war between King Stephen and Empress Matilda over succession to Henry I.
Medieval confederation of North German and Baltic trading cities dominating northern European commerce.
Crusading military order that conquered and Christianized Prussia and the Baltic, defeated by Poland-Lithuania at Grunwald.
Mid-14th-century plague pandemic that killed roughly a third of Europe's population, reshaping its economy and society.
Central European kingdom centered on Prague, home to the Hussite movement and later Habsburg rule after 1526.
Dualist Christian heretical movement in southern France, suppressed by the papally sanctioned Albigensian Crusade.
1096-1099 campaign called by Urban II that captured Jerusalem and established Crusader states in the Levant.
Likely the English king whose weak reign saw the Second Barons' War and Parliament's rise under Simon de Montfort.
Possibly King John of England, who lost Normandy to France and was forced to seal Magna Carta in 1215.
Southern Italian kingdom ruled successively by Normans, Angevins, Aragonese, and Spanish Habsburgs.
French city that hosted the papacy from 1309 to 1377 during the "Babylonian Captivity" of the Church.
Medieval Central European kingdom founded by Stephen I, later ruled by Ottomans and Habsburgs.
Centuries-long Christian reconquest of Muslim Iberia, completed by Ferdinand and Isabella's capture of Granada in 1492.
Balkan people traditionally linked to the ancient Illyrians, later resisting Ottoman rule under Skanderbeg.
Iberian kingdom whose Mediterranean empire and union with Castile under Ferdinand and Isabella forged modern Spain.
Wealthy duchess who married Louis VII of France then Henry II of England, mother of Richard I and John.
1204 crusade diverted to sack Christian Constantinople, fatally weakening the Byzantine Empire.
Crusading military order and early banking network, suppressed and its leader Jacques de Molay burned under Philip IV of France.
Northern British kingdom that won independence from England under Robert the Bruce before its 1707 union with England.
Early-13th-century papal crusade against the Cathar heretics of southern France, led by Simon de Montfort.
Holy Roman Emperor of the Hohenstaufen dynasty who battled the Lombard League and drowned on the Third Crusade.
Later canonized French king who led the Seventh and Eighth Crusades and codified French royal justice.
Former Visigothic and Moorish Spanish capital, noted for its medieval school of translation and religious coexistence.
English king defeated by Robert the Bruce at Bannockburn, later deposed and murdered amid baronial opposition.
English king who won the Battle of Agincourt during the Hundred Years' War and was celebrated by Shakespeare.
Elite Ottoman infantry corps recruited via the devshirme levy of Christian boys, eventually abolished in 1826.
Former royal capital of Poland, seat of the Jagiellonian University and Wawel Castle.
English proto-Protestant followers of John Wycliffe who challenged Church authority and translated the Bible.
First historic Polish royal dynasty, beginning with Mieszko I, who converted Poland to Christianity.
English king who suppressed the Peasants' Revolt as a boy but was later deposed by Henry Bolingbroke.
1147-1149 crusade led by Louis VII and Conrad III that failed to recapture Edessa from Muslim forces.
1189-1192 crusade led by Richard I, Philip II, and Frederick Barbarossa against Saladin, failing to retake Jerusalem.
Senior cleric of the English Church, an office held by Thomas Becket, murdered after conflict with Henry II.
Catalan capital and medieval Mediterranean trading power within the Crown of Aragon.

entries.topic: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
entries.answerline: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
entries.note: 1212 battle in which a Christian coalition decisively defeated the Almohads, turning the tide of the Reconquista.
Last Valois Duke of Burgundy, whose ambitions to build a middle kingdom ended with his death at Nancy in 1477.
Andalusian city, capital of the Umayyad Caliphate of Cordoba, famed for its Great Mosque and religious tolerance.
English king who claimed the French throne, starting the Hundred Years' War, and won at Crecy.
Likely Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor and "Stupor Mundi," a Sicilian-based ruler who clashed repeatedly with the papacy.
Mongol khanate that dominated Russia and the steppe, exacting tribute from Rus' princes for over two centuries.
Possibly the Holy Roman Emperor who stood barefoot at Canossa during the Investiture Controversy with Pope Gregory VII.
Weak English king during the Hundred Years' War's end and the Wars of the Roses, deposed and later murdered.
Crusading military and charitable order that later held Rhodes and then Malta against Ottoman sieges.
French "Spider King" who consolidated royal power and defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy.
Leading city of the Hanseatic League, dominating medieval Baltic and North Sea trade.
French city whose 1429 siege was lifted by Joan of Arc, turning the tide of the Hundred Years' War.
Welsh nobleman who led a major early-15th-century revolt against English rule, briefly ruling an independent Wales.
Scottish king who won independence from England at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314.
Founding royal dynasty of Hungary, beginning with the conquest under Arpad and ending with Andrew III in 1301.
1214 battle in which Philip II of France defeated a coalition including King John of England and the Holy Roman Emperor.
1356 Hundred Years' War battle in which the Black Prince captured the French king John II.
French port city held by England for two centuries after Edward III's siege, lost under Mary I.
Central Iberian kingdom whose union with Aragon under Ferdinand and Isabella forged modern Spain.
Historic region split between Spain's Christian kingdom and, in Eastern Europe, Poland and later Austria-Hungary.
Angevin English king who built a vast continental empire and clashed with Thomas Becket over Church authority.
First Tudor king who won the crown at Bosworth Field, ending the Wars of the Roses.
Decentralized Central European empire founded by Otto the Great, lasting from 962 until Napoleon dissolved it in 1806.
Bohemian reformer burned at the stake for heresy, whose execution sparked the Hussite Wars.
English "Lionheart" king who led the Third Crusade against Saladin and spent little time in England.
First king of Hungary, canonized as a saint, who Christianized the country around the year 1000.
Historic region contested by Hungary, the Ottomans, and Romania, home to Vlad the Impaler and Dracula legends.
Castilian king called "the Wise," who sponsored astronomical tables and a landmark legal code.
Ancient Syrian city captured during the First Crusade, becoming one of the Crusader states' principalities.
1385 battle securing Portuguese independence from Castile with English longbow support.
Pope who clashed with Philip IV of France over royal authority, leading to his humiliation at Anagni.
Celtic-influenced French duchy that retained autonomy until its union with the French crown in 1532.
Northeastern Iberian region within the Crown of Aragon, with a modern separatist movement seeking independence from Spain.
German Rhineland city and archbishopric, home to a great Gothic cathedral housing relics of the Magi.
Tatar successor state to the Golden Horde on the Black Sea, an Ottoman vassal until Russian annexation in 1783.
English prince who won major Hundred Years' War victories at Crecy and Poitiers but died before inheriting the throne.
English queen consort called the "She-Wolf," who deposed her husband Edward II with her lover Mortimer.
Muscovite grand prince called "the Great," who ended Mongol tribute and tripled Russian territory.
1358 violent peasant uprising in northern France during the Hundred Years' War's chaos.
Last Grand Master of the Knights Templar, burned at the stake after Philip IV suppressed the order.
Polish-Lithuanian royal dynasty founded by Jogaila, ruling a vast Central European union into the 16th century.
French peasant girl who claimed divine visions, lifted the siege of Orleans, and was burned as a heretic.
Southern German duchy, later kingdom, long ruled by the Wittelsbach dynasty from its capital Munich.
Central European kingdom of the Piast and Jagiellonian dynasties, later partitioned out of existence in the 18th century.
Baltic state that formed a vast medieval union with Poland under the Jagiellonians, independent again since 1990.
Empress and claimant to the English throne whose civil war with King Stephen was called the Anarchy.
Ottoman sultan called "the Conqueror," who captured Constantinople in 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire.
Legendary Christian king believed by medieval Europeans to rule a powerful kingdom in Asia or Africa.
Muslim sultan who recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187, prompting the Third Crusade against him.
Southern Italian city famed for Europe's earliest medical school, later a WWII Allied landing site.
Mediterranean island contested by Pisa, Aragon, and Savoy, later giving its name to the Kingdom that unified Italy.
German duchy and later electorate, home to the Wettin dynasty and a major early Reformation stronghold.
English baron who led rebellion against Henry III and summoned an early representative Parliament.
Late-15th-century English civil wars between the houses of Lancaster and York, ended by Henry Tudor's victory.
Scottish knight who defeated the English at Stirling Bridge before his capture and execution.
Northern English city and namesake of the House of York in the Wars of the Roses.
Crusader stronghold in the Levant, the last mainland Christian foothold to fall in 1291.
Novgorodian prince who defeated Swedish and Teutonic Knight invaders while paying tribute to the Mongols.
1492 edict by Ferdinand and Isabella expelling Jews who refused conversion from Spain.
Iberian people with a distinct pre-Indo-European language, straddling the Spanish-French border in the Pyrenees.
1415 battle in which Henry V's outnumbered English longbowmen crushed the French during the Hundred Years' War.
1410 battle in which Poland-Lithuania decisively defeated the Teutonic Knights.
1389 battle in which the Ottomans defeated Serbian forces, becoming central to Serbian national mythology.
1302 battle in which Flemish militia infantry defeated French knightly cavalry.
Flemish trading city, a leading medieval European commercial and cloth-manufacturing center.
Reformed Benedictine monastic order founded at Citeaux, known for austere discipline and agricultural innovation.
Scottish capital built around its historic castle, seat of Scottish monarchy and later Enlightenment learning.
Yorkist king who won the throne during the Wars of the Roses, father of the Princes in the Tower.
Claimant to the English throne whose civil war with King Stephen was known as the Anarchy.
Wealthy medieval Low Countries region famed for cloth manufacturing, later a bloody World War I front.
Vast medieval state that united with Poland under the Jagiellonians, once Europe's largest by area.
Weak English king during the Hundred Years' War's end and the Wars of the Roses, deposed and later murdered.
11th-12th-century conflict between popes and emperors over who could appoint bishops, climaxing at Canossa.
French medieval chronicler whose Chronicles vividly narrate the Hundred Years' War.
Holy city captured by the First Crusade in 1099 and recaptured by Saladin in 1187.
Central European kingdom centered on Prague, home to the Hussite movement and later Habsburg rule after 1526.
Southern Italian kingdom ruled successively by Normans, Angevins, Aragonese, and Spanish Habsburgs.
Small Pyrenean kingdom straddling Spain and France, eventually absorbed by both Bourbon crowns.
Northern British kingdom that won independence from England under Robert the Bruce before its 1707 union with England.
Major French city and early Reformation-era printing center on the Rhone River.
Formal name of Magna Carta, the 1215 charter limiting King John's power and asserting baronial rights.
Likely Philip IV of France, who suppressed the Templars and clashed with Pope Boniface VIII.
French king called "the Fair," who suppressed the Knights Templar and humiliated Pope Boniface VIII.
Frankish legal tradition barring female succession, invoked to justify the Hundred Years' War's succession dispute.
Albanian nobleman who led decades of resistance against Ottoman conquest in the 15th century.
English town, site of early Wars of the Roses battles and home to a major medieval abbey.
Crusading military order that conquered and Christianized Prussia and the Baltic, defeated at Grunwald.
Leader of the 1381 Peasants' Revolt against English poll taxes, killed after confronting Richard II.
Border region between England and Wales ruled by semi-autonomous Marcher lords after the Norman Conquest.
French royal advisor and abbot whose rebuilding of Saint-Denis pioneered the Gothic architectural style.
Sea bordering Venice's maritime empire and later a contested World War I and Yugoslav-era frontier.
German margrave who founded the Margraviate of Brandenburg through conquest of Slavic lands.
French county and namesake of the Angevin dynasty that ruled England under Henry II and his sons.
Iberian people with a distinct pre-Indo-European language, straddling the Spanish-French border in the Pyrenees.
1346 battle where English longbowmen devastated French cavalry early in the Hundred Years' War.
Mongol commander and grandson of Genghis Khan who founded the Golden Horde and devastated Rus'.
Influential Cistercian abbot who preached the Second Crusade and shaped medieval monastic reform.
English cathedral city where Archbishop Thomas Becket was murdered after conflict with Henry II.
French royal dynasty founded by Hugh Capet in 987, ruling in direct line until 1328.
French region famed for its medieval trade fairs and later its sparkling wine.
French king crowned with Joan of Arc's help, who ultimately expelled the English from France.
1122 agreement ending the Investiture Controversy between the papacy and the Holy Roman Emperor.
1414-1418 church council that ended the Papal Schism and had Jan Hus burned for heresy.
Celtic-influenced French duchy that retained autonomy until its union with the French crown in 1532.
Crusader county in the Levant, the first Crusader state to fall, prompting the Second Crusade.
Kingdom unified by the Anglo-Saxons and conquered by the Normans in 1066, later joined with Scotland and Wales.
Medieval Italian faction supporting the Holy Roman Emperor against the pro-papal Guelphs.
1356 decree of Charles IV formalizing the election procedure for the Holy Roman Emperor.
Hungarian mercenary who led a bloody 1514 peasant revolt, crushed and executed by the nobility.
Medieval dynasty that provided several Holy Roman Emperors, including Charles IV, and Bohemian kings.
French royal dynasty ruling from 1328 to 1589, spanning the Hundred Years' War and Italian Wars.
First Capetian king of France, elected in 987, founding a dynasty that ruled in direct line until 1328.
Powerful medieval pope who called the Fourth Crusade and the Albigensian Crusade, asserting papal supremacy.
Hungarian general who defended Christian Europe against Ottoman advances, notably at Belgrade in 1456.
Iberian kingdom whose Mediterranean empire and union with Castile under Ferdinand and Isabella forged modern Spain.
Mediterranean island kingdom ruled successively by Normans, Hohenstaufens, Angevins, and Aragonese.
Boy pretender crowned "Edward VI" by Yorkist rebels against Henry VII, later pardoned to work in the royal kitchen.
Baltic region conquered and Christianized by the Livonian and Teutonic Knights, later contested by regional powers.
Coalition of English nobles who curbed Richard II's power and executed his favorites in the 1380s.
Medieval German march that grew into the core of Prussia under the Hohenzollern dynasty.
Pope elected at the Council of Constance in 1417, ending the Western Schism.
Renaissance-era Hungarian king known for his Black Army and patronage of humanist learning.
Central Asian steppe empire whose Golden Horde branch devastated and dominated Rus' for centuries.
Small Pyrenean kingdom straddling Spain and France, eventually absorbed by both Bourbon crowns.
Sicilian capital that blended Norman, Arab, and Byzantine cultures under the Kingdom of Sicily.
1381 English uprising against poll taxes, led by Wat Tyler, that reached and threatened London.
Pretender who claimed to be a surviving Prince in the Tower, challenging Henry VII's throne.
Favorite of Edward II whose influence provoked baronial opposition and led to his execution.
Medieval Italian maritime republic and trade rival of Genoa and Venice, famed for its leaning tower.
Baltic coastal region contested among Sweden, Brandenburg-Prussia, and Poland across centuries.
Dalmatian maritime city-state, modern Dubrovnik, that balanced Venetian and Ottoman power through diplomacy.
Major Central European river long serving as a cultural and political border, especially with France.
Aegean island held by the Knights Hospitaller until the Ottomans conquered it in 1522.
Last Plantagenet king, killed at Bosworth Field, allegedly responsible for the Princes in the Tower's deaths.

entries.topic: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester
entries.answerline: Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester
entries.note: English baron who led rebellion against Henry III and summoned an early representative Parliament.
Author of a fantastical medieval travelogue describing distant lands, likely based on other travelers' accounts.
1302 papal bull by Boniface VIII asserting supreme papal authority over temporal rulers.
English university founded in 1209 by scholars who left Oxford after a town-gown dispute.
Medieval Europe's leading center of theological and philosophical learning, chartered in the 12th century.
Baltic and North Sea privateers-turned-pirates active in the late 14th century, opposing the Hanseatic League.
Capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, later contested between Poland, Russia, and the Soviet Union.
Gotland trading town, a major Hanseatic League center, sacked by Denmark's Valdemar IV in 1361.
Grand Duke of Lithuania who expanded the state to its greatest extent, defeating the Teutonic Knights at Grunwald.
Medieval Christian reform movement founded by Peter Waldo, condemned as heretical and persecuted for centuries.
London district housing the Abbey and Parliament, the seat of English monarchy and governance.
Only English pope, who reputedly granted Ireland to Henry II via the disputed Laudabiliter bull.
Southern Portuguese region, the last area reconquered from Muslim rule during the Reconquista.
Long-standing Scotland-France alliance against England, dating from the late 13th century.
1471 Wars of the Roses battle in which Edward IV defeated and killed the Earl of Warwick.
Legendary early-8th-century battle marking the start of the Christian Reconquista in Iberia.
1380 battle in which Russian forces under Dmitri Donskoi defeated the Golden Horde's army.
1367 battle in which the Black Prince restored Pedro the Cruel to Castile's throne during a Hundred Years' War side conflict.
1396 crusade battle in which the Ottomans crushed a European coalition, ending major anti-Ottoman crusading.
1242 battle in which Alexander Nevsky's forces defeated the Teutonic Knights on a frozen lake.
Borgia pope who called for crusades against the Ottomans after the fall of Constantinople.
Dualist Christian heretical movement in southern France, suppressed by the Albigensian Crusade.
Holy Roman Emperor and King of Bohemia who issued the Golden Bull and made Prague an imperial capital.
1212 popular movement of youths said to have marched toward the Holy Land, ending in tragedy or legend.
Armenian kingdom in Anatolia allied with the Crusader states against Muslim powers.
Pope who moved the papacy to Avignon and suppressed the Knights Templar under pressure from Philip IV.
Highly influential Burgundian Benedictine monastery that spearheaded medieval monastic reform.
Last Byzantine emperor, who died defending Constantinople against the Ottomans in 1453.
Last Byzantine emperor, who died defending Constantinople against the Ottomans in 1453.
Southwestern English peninsula with a distinct Celtic heritage and historic tin-mining economy.
Series of religious wars from 1095 to 1291 aimed at capturing and holding Jerusalem for Christendom.
Venetian doge family, most notably Enrico Dandolo, who directed the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople.
German duchy central to Ottonian power, later fragmenting into electorates including Saxe-Wittenberg.
Castilian nobleman and mercenary who fought both for and against Muslim rulers during the Reconquista.
1454 lavish Burgundian banquet where nobles vowed a crusade against the Ottomans after Constantinople's fall.
1217-1221 crusade that attacked Egypt but failed to trade it for Jerusalem's return.
Sicilian-based emperor and "Stupor Mundi" who clashed repeatedly with the papacy and led the Sixth Crusade.
Traditional regional charters of rights and privileges in medieval Spanish kingdoms, notably the Basque Country.
Last Muslim emirate in Iberia, conquered by Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492, completing the Reconquista.
Medieval Italian faction supporting the papacy against the pro-imperial Ghibellines.
Most powerful medieval Welsh kingdom, whose princes led resistance against English conquest.
Norman king whose death without a male heir triggered the succession crisis known as the Anarchy.
French comital dynasty whose branches ruled England (as Plantagenets) and later Naples and Hungary.
Magyar people who settled the Carpathian Basin and founded the medieval Kingdom of Hungary.
1420s-1430s conflicts pitting followers of executed reformer Jan Hus against Catholic crusading forces.
Former Constantinople, renamed after the Ottoman conquest of 1453 and made the empire's capital.
Blind Hussite military commander whose innovative wagon-fort tactics repeatedly defeated crusading armies.
Lithuanian grand duke who became King of Poland, founding the Jagiellonian dynasty via marriage.
Scottish king installed by Edward I of England, later forced to abdicate amid the Wars of Scottish Independence.

entries.topic: John of Gaunt, 1st Duke of Lancaster
entries.answerline: John of Gaunt, 1st Duke of Lancaster
entries.note: Powerful son of Edward III who dominated English politics and fathered the Lancastrian royal line.
English theologian whose Bible translation and challenge to Church authority inspired the Lollards.
Central Iberian kingdom whose union with Aragon under Ferdinand and Isabella forged modern Spain.
Crusader state established after the First Crusade in 1099, lost to Saladin in 1187.
Western European Catholics as termed by Byzantines, especially the crusaders who sacked Constantinople in 1204.
Coalition of northern Italian city-states that defeated Frederick Barbarossa's imperial forces at Legnano.
Northern Italian region named for the Lombards, later contested by Austria and unified Italy.
Major French city and early Reformation-era printing center on the Rhone River.
1215 charter forced on King John by rebellious barons, limiting royal power and asserting legal rights.
Venetian merchant traveler whose account of his journey to Mongol-ruled China shaped European views of Asia.
Queen who engineered the Kalmar Union joining Denmark, Norway, and Sweden under one crown.
Flemish city that served as capital of the Habsburg Netherlands under Margaret of Austria.
Byzantine emperor who recaptured Constantinople from the Latins in 1261, restoring Byzantine rule.
First and only crowned king of Lithuania, who briefly converted to Christianity for political advantage.
Central European region home to the early medieval Great Moravian state, later part of Bohemia.
Roger Mortimer, lover of Isabella of France, who helped depose Edward II before his own execution.
Byzantine successor state and exile capital after the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople.
Northern European sea central to Viking, Hanseatic, and later British and Dutch maritime power.
Last Byzantine imperial dynasty, restoring the empire in 1261 until its fall to the Ottomans in 1453.
Territories in central Italy ruled directly by the pope until absorbed by unified Italy in 1870.
Wealthy Greek families of Constantinople's Phanar district who served as Ottoman administrators.
French king who greatly expanded royal territory, defeating King John's coalition at Bouvines.
Duke of Burgundy whose wealthy court patronized the arts and founded the Order of the Golden Fleece.
West Slavic people whose medieval kingdom under the Piasts and Jagiellonians shaped Central Europe.
Mountain range dividing Spain and France, home to the Basque people and site of the Roland legend.
1290 English statute restricting subinfeudation, reshaping feudal land tenure.
Dalmatian maritime city-state, modern Dubrovnik, that balanced Venetian and Ottoman power through diplomacy.
Baltic Hanseatic League city founded by German crusaders, later capital of Latvia.
Norman adventurer who conquered southern Italy and fought the Byzantine Empire.
Legendary English outlaw said to rob the rich to aid the poor during the reign of a wayward king.
Central Asian Silk Road city that became the glittering capital of Timur's Central Asian empire.
Medieval English tax paid by knights in lieu of military service to the crown.
English king whose disputed reign against Empress Matilda plunged the country into the civil war called the Anarchy.
First French pope, renowned scholar who introduced Arabic numerals and the astrolabe to Western Europe.
Petty Muslim successor kingdoms that fragmented Iberia after the Umayyad Caliphate of Cordoba's collapse.
Georgian queen who presided over her kingdom's medieval golden age of territorial and cultural expansion.
Norman fortress-palace-prison on the Thames, associated with the murder of the Princes in the Tower.
Pope who launched the First Crusade with his 1095 call at the Council of Clermont.
Mediterranean Spanish kingdom conquered from Muslim rule by El Cid and later the Crown of Aragon.
Wallachian prince notorious for impaling enemies, later the inspiration for Bram Stoker's vampire novel.
Romanian principality ruled by figures like Vlad the Impaler, a Danubian buffer against Ottoman expansion.
Irish port town, site of a Norman landing and later Cromwell's brutal 1649 siege.

sections.name: Renaissance and the Age of Exploration
sections.blurb: The Italian city-states and humanism, the invention of printing, and the Portuguese and Spanish voyages of discovery, roughly 1400-1600.

entries.note: Iberian kingdom that pioneered Atlantic exploration under Henry the Navigator and Vasco da Gama; later Salazar's Estado Novo.
Tuscan city-state ruled by the Medici, cradle of the Italian Renaissance in art, banking, and humanist thought.
Maritime republic ruled by an elected doge, dominant in Mediterranean trade and a major Renaissance art center.
Italian maritime republic and trade rival of Venice, birthplace of Christopher Columbus.
Portuguese capital and launch point for Vasco da Gama's voyages, devastated by the catastrophic 1755 earthquake.
Lombard city-state ruled by the Visconti and Sforza dukes, a major Renaissance power contested by France and Spain.
1571 naval battle in which a Holy League fleet decisively defeated the Ottoman navy in the Gulf of Corinth.
French Renaissance king and rival of Charles V, patron of Leonardo da Vinci, captured at the Battle of Pavia.
Possibly Isabella I of Castile, who united Spain with Ferdinand, funded Columbus, and completed the Reconquista.
German printer whose movable-type press revolutionized the spread of texts, first mass-producing the Gutenberg Bible.
Warrior pope who commissioned Michelangelo's Sistine ceiling and Raphael's Vatican frescoes while waging war for papal lands.
Ottoman sultan at the empire's peak, who besieged Vienna and captured Belgrade and Rhodes.
Spanish friar and chronicler of the Americas whose writings denounced colonial abuses against indigenous peoples.
1526 battle in which the Ottomans crushed Hungary, killing its king and opening the way to Ottoman dominance.
Genoese explorer whose 1492 Spanish-sponsored voyage reached the Americas, opening the Columbian Exchange.
Portuguese royal dynasty that restored independence from Spain in 1640 and ruled until the 1910 republic.
Iberian kingdom that pioneered Atlantic exploration under Henry the Navigator before becoming a republic in 1910.
Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor whose marriage alliances brought Burgundy and Spain into Habsburg hands.
Borgia pope notorious for nepotism and corruption, father of Cesare and Lucrezia Borgia.
Genoese admiral and statesman who dominated Mediterranean naval affairs in the 16th century.
1525 battle in which Charles V's forces captured Francis I of France, a major Habsburg-Valois turning point.
French king whose 1494 invasion of Italy launched the decades-long Italian Wars.
Italian noble house that produced Pope Paul III and ruled the Duchy of Parma.
Portuguese royal dynasty that presided over the Age of Exploration under Henry the Navigator.
Florentine banking dynasty that ruled Tuscany and produced several popes, patronizing Renaissance art.
Milanese ducal dynasty that patronized Leonardo da Vinci before losing power to France and Spain.
German city where Johannes Gutenberg developed movable-type printing in the mid-15th century.
Medieval and early modern maritime republic, trade rival of Venice and birthplace of Christopher Columbus.
Maritime republic ruled by an elected doge, dominant in Mediterranean trade and a major Renaissance art center.
English privateer who circumnavigated the globe and helped defeat the Spanish Armada.
Sexually transmitted disease that spread rapidly through Europe after Columbus's voyages, devastating populations.
Italian city home to Europe's oldest university, founded in 1088.
Spanish-Italian noble family producing Pope Alexander VI and his children Cesare and Lucrezia, notorious for intrigue.
Mercenary military captains hired by Italian city-states during the Renaissance's frequent wars.
French Renaissance king and rival of Charles V, patron of Leonardo da Vinci, captured at the Battle of Pavia.
Tuscan city-state and rival of Florence, famed for its distinctive Gothic art and the Palio horse race.
1513 battle in which English forces killed Scotland's King James IV and much of its nobility.
1515 battle where Francis I's French forces defeated the Swiss during the Italian Wars.
Ruthless son of Pope Alexander VI, a model for Machiavelli's The Prince.
German banking dynasty that financed Habsburg emperors and dominated European high finance.
Florentine friar who denounced Medici excess, ruled briefly via the "Bonfire of the Vanities," and was burned as a heretic.
Portuguese prince who sponsored early Atlantic voyages down the African coast, launching the Age of Exploration.
Italian explorer sailing for England who reached North America in 1497.
Elizabethan mathematician, astrologer, and advisor who blended science with occult study.
Florentine banking dynasty that ruled Tuscany and produced several popes, patronizing Renaissance art.
Northern Italian city near which Charles V's forces captured Francis I of France in 1525.
1478 failed plot by a rival Florentine family to assassinate the Medici brothers.
1520-1521 Castilian urban revolt against the young Charles V's centralizing rule.
Portuguese explorer who opened the first sea route from Europe to India around Africa.

entries.topic: War of the League of Cambrai
entries.answerline: War of the League of Cambrai
entries.note: Early-16th-century Italian war pitting a shifting coalition of powers against the Republic of Venice.
Devastating sack of Rome by mutinous Habsburg troops, effectively ending the High Renaissance there.
French Renaissance surgeon who pioneered battlefield wound treatment and surgical instruments.
Notorious 1501 Vatican party allegedly hosted by Cesare Borgia, emblematic of Borgia-era papal scandal.
Atlantic archipelago conquered by Castile, serving as a launch point for transatlantic voyages.
Secretive Venetian magistracy responsible for state security and surveillance in the republic.
Dutch Christian humanist scholar whose satirical and reformist writings preceded but did not join the Reformation.
Elected head of state of the Venetian Republic, holding largely ceremonial but prestigious authority.
Portuguese explorer whose expedition achieved the first circumnavigation of the globe, though he died en route.
Italian Renaissance city-state ruled by the Este family, a noted center of art and learning.
Indian coastal territory seized by Portugal in 1510, a key Estado da India colonial hub.
Italian noble family that ruled Mantua as Renaissance patrons of the arts.
Italian noble family that ruled Mantua as Renaissance patrons of the arts.
Castilian queen who united Spain with Ferdinand, funded Columbus, and completed the Reconquista.
Castilian queen whose mental instability and confinement let her father and son rule in her name.
Hungarian noble who claimed the crown after Mohacs, ruling with Ottoman backing against Habsburg rivals.
1526 anti-Habsburg alliance of Italian and French powers formed against Charles V.
Medici pope whose indulgence sales to fund St. Peter's Basilica helped provoke Luther's Reformation.
Milanese duke and patron of Leonardo da Vinci whose invitation of France into Italy backfired disastrously.
Italian city-state ruled by the Gonzaga family as Renaissance patrons of the arts.
French peasant whose disputed identity case, involving an impostor claiming to be him, became a famous legal drama.
Central sea of trade and conflict among Venice, Genoa, the Ottomans, and Habsburg Spain.
Ottoman chief architect under Suleiman the Magnificent, designer of the Suleymaniye Mosque.
Formal name of Venice, the maritime republic ruled by an elected doge and dominant in Mediterranean trade.
Gutenberg's movable-type invention that revolutionized the spread of texts across Europe.
Elizabethan courtier, explorer, and colonizer who founded the failed Roanoke colony, later executed by James I.
Cultural rebirth beginning in 14th-century Italy, reviving classical learning and transforming art and thought.

sections.name: Reformation and the Wars of Religion
sections.blurb: Luther, Calvin, and Zwingli's challenge to Rome, the Catholic Counter-Reformation, the French Wars of Religion, the Thirty Years' War, and Tudor-Stuart England, roughly 1500-1660.

entries.note: Alpine confederation of cantons, home to Zwingli's and Calvin's Reformation and long-standing armed political neutrality.
Low Countries state that won independence from Habsburg Spain in the Eighty Years' War, then flourished in its Golden Age.
1618-1648 conflict sparked by the Defenestration of Prague, devastating Germany before ending with the Peace of Westphalia.
Puritan general who led Parliament to victory in the English Civil War, had Charles I executed, and ruled as Lord Protector.
Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor and King of Spain who ruled a vast empire and confronted Martin Luther at the Diet of Worms.
Iberian kingdom unified under Ferdinand and Isabella, a Counter-Reformation superpower under the Habsburgs before later decline.
Swiss city that became Calvin's Protestant theocratic base, later home to the League of Nations and Red Cross.
Swedish king and "Lion of the North" whose Thirty Years' War intervention revived the Protestant cause before his death at Lutzen.
German monk whose Ninety-Five Theses launched the Protestant Reformation; defended his views at the Diet of Worms.
Likely Charles II of England, the "Merry Monarch" restored to the throne in 1660 after Cromwell's Commonwealth.
Tudor queen who restored Protestantism, defeated the Spanish Armada, and presided over England's Elizabethan golden age.
First tsar of Russia who conquered Kazan and Astrakhan but plunged the realm into terror via the Oprichnina.
Iberian kingdom unified under Ferdinand and Isabella, a Counter-Reformation superpower under the Habsburgs before later decline.
Flemish port city and 16th-century commercial hub, sacked during the Dutch Revolt's "Spanish Fury."
Likely Philip II of Spain, Habsburg king who sent the Armada against England and fought the Dutch Revolt.
English king whose conflict with Parliament sparked the Civil War, ending in his 1649 execution.
Tudor king who broke with Rome to annul his marriage, founding the Church of England; married six wives.
French Protestants persecuted during the Wars of Religion and after Louis XIV revoked the Edict of Nantes.
Habsburg king who sent the Armada against England, fought the Dutch Revolt, and defended Catholicism across Europe.
German city that gave its name to the 1555 religious peace and the 1530 Lutheran confession of faith.
Dutch royal house descended from William the Silent, leader of the revolt against Habsburg Spain.
German city notoriously sacked and largely destroyed by Catholic League forces during the Thirty Years' War.
Ottoman-origin flower whose Dutch speculative bubble in the 1630s is a famed early financial mania.
Swiss city where Huldrych Zwingli launched the Swiss Reformation in the 1520s.
1632 Thirty Years' War battle where Gustavus Adolphus's Swedish forces won but the king himself was killed.
First wife of Henry VIII of England, whose annulment battle precipitated England's break with Rome.
Italian astronomer whose telescopic observations supporting Copernican heliocentrism led to his trial by the Inquisition.
French Protestant reformer whose theology of predestination shaped Geneva's theocracy and Reformed Christianity.
Catholic queen of Scotland executed by her cousin Elizabeth I after implication in plots against her.
Church-state tribunal founded by Ferdinand and Isabella to enforce Catholic orthodoxy, targeting conversos and Moriscos.
Founder of Dutch independence who led the revolt against Habsburg Spain before his assassination in 1584.
Dutch trading capital that flourished during the 17th-century Golden Age, home to the first stock exchange.
Radical Reformation movement rejecting infant baptism, notorious for the violent Munster Rebellion of the 1530s.
Boy king of England, Henry VIII's son, whose short reign pushed the Church of England toward Protestantism.
Likely the Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor whose Counter-Reformation zeal helped ignite the Thirty Years' War.
1524-1525 mass uprising of German peasants demanding social and religious reform, brutally suppressed; condemned by Luther.
Swedish royal dynasty founded by Gustav Vasa, which also ruled Poland-Lithuania for a time.
Catholic religious order founded by Ignatius of Loyola, central to the Counter-Reformation and global missionary work.
Spanish Muslims forcibly converted to Christianity, later expelled from Spain in the early 17th century.
1536 popular uprising in northern England against Henry VIII's dissolution of the monasteries.
Western Christian church whose authority was challenged by the Reformation and reasserted at the Council of Trent.
1588 fleet sent by Philip II of Spain to invade England, defeated by English ships and storms.
Swedish royal dynasty founded by Gustav Vasa, which also ruled Poland-Lithuania for a time.
1620 battle opening the Thirty Years' War, where Catholic forces crushed Bohemian Protestant rebels near Prague.
1598 decree by Henry IV granting Huguenots religious toleration, later revoked by Louis XIV in 1685.
1642-1651 conflict between Parliament and Charles I, resulting in the king's execution and Cromwell's Commonwealth.
Parliamentarian general and Cromwell's son-in-law, a key figure in the Putney Debates and Charles I's trial.
First Bourbon king who ended the Wars of Religion with the Edict of Nantes after converting to Catholicism.
Firebrand Scottish reformer who founded Presbyterianism and clashed fiercely with Mary, Queen of Scots.
Radical English Civil War-era movement demanding expanded suffrage and legal equality, voiced in the Putney Debates.
Catholic Tudor queen called "Bloody Mary" for burning Protestants while trying to reverse England's Reformation.
1648 treaties ending the Thirty Years' War, establishing state sovereignty and religious settlement in the Holy Roman Empire.
Andalusian city that monopolized Spain's American trade through its Casa de Contratacion.
Alsatian city and early Reformation center, later contested between France and Germany for centuries.
1540s English military campaign to force a marriage alliance with Mary, Queen of Scots.
German city where Martin Luther defended his teachings before Charles V at the 1521 Diet.
Second wife of Henry VIII, whose marriage prompted England's break with Rome; executed on adultery charges.
1645 decisive Parliamentarian victory over Charles I's forces in the English Civil War.
Florentine-born French queen mother who shaped policy during the Wars of Religion, blamed for the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre.
England's "Nine Days' Queen," installed to block Catholic Mary I's accession and later executed.
Dutch university city that withstood a famous Spanish siege during the Eighty Years' War.
Spanish capital established by Philip II, seat of the Habsburg and later Bourbon monarchy.
French city that gave its name to Henry IV's 1598 edict of religious toleration.
Western Christian faith whose authority was challenged by the Reformation and reasserted at the Council of Trent.
1572 mass killing of Huguenots in Paris, ordered amid the French Wars of Religion.
Archbishop of Canterbury who shaped the English Reformation, burned at the stake under Mary I.
1534 Protestant anti-Catholic poster campaign in France that triggered a harsh royal crackdown.
Dutch city whose sieges were pivotal in the Eighty Years' War against Spain.
Spanish Atlantic port, target of Sir Francis Drake's 1587 raid and a base for New World trade.
Danish king whose intervention in the Thirty Years' War ended in defeat by Catholic forces.
1545-1563 Catholic council that defined Counter-Reformation doctrine against Protestant challenges.
1568-1648 Dutch revolt against Habsburg Spain, ending in recognized independence at Westphalia.
Favorite of James I and Charles I whose disastrous foreign policy led to his 1628 assassination.
Swiss reformer who launched Zurich's Reformation, dying in battle against Catholic cantons.
First Stuart king of England, also James VI of Scotland, who commissioned the King James Bible.
First Stuart king of England, also James VI of Scotland, who commissioned the King James Bible.
Catholic religious order founded by Ignatius of Loyola, central to the Counter-Reformation and global missions.
English author of the Book of Martyrs, chronicling Protestants persecuted under Mary I.
Parliamentarian general during the English Civil War who helped topple the Rump Parliament.
French Huguenot stronghold besieged and starved into surrender by Cardinal Richelieu in 1628.
German city seized by radical Anabaptists in the 1530s before a brutal siege crushed their theocracy.
Parliamentarian force reorganized by Cromwell that won the English Civil War.
Swiss-German Renaissance physician who pioneered chemical medicine, rejecting classical medical authority.
Habsburg king whose long reign saw Spain's continued decline despite Velazquez's court patronage.
Swedish monarch who abdicated and converted to Catholicism, ending direct Vasa succession.
1571 failed conspiracy to assassinate Elizabeth I and install Mary, Queen of Scots.
English Parliament remnant that tried and executed Charles I after Pride's Purge.
Chief executive office of the Dutch Republic, typically held by a Prince of Orange.
Radical English Civil War-era agrarian communists who occupied common land for collective farming.
Phase of the French Wars of Religion pitting Henry III, Henry of Navarre, and Henry of Guise against each other.
1631 Thirty Years' War battle in which Gustavus Adolphus's Swedish army won a major Protestant victory.
1642 inconclusive first major battle of the English Civil War.
1634 Thirty Years' War battle in which Habsburg forces crushed the Swedish army.
Standard Anglican liturgy compiled by Thomas Cranmer during the English Reformation.
Henry VIII's powerful chief minister whose failure to secure the king's annulment led to his downfall.
English "Merry Monarch" restored to the throne in 1660 after Cromwell's Commonwealth.
Henry VIII's 1536-1541 seizure and closure of England's monastic institutions and wealth.
Spanish general whose brutal repression as governor of the Netherlands helped ignite the Dutch Revolt.
Spanish nobleman who commanded the ill-fated Armada against England in 1588.
Low Countries state that won independence from Habsburg Spain in the Eighty Years' War, then flourished in its Golden Age.
1629 Habsburg decree reclaiming Protestant-secularized Church lands, intensifying the Thirty Years' War.
English jurist who championed common law supremacy over royal prerogative under James I and Charles I.
Tudor queen who restored Protestantism, defeated the Spanish Armada, and presided over England's Elizabethan golden age.
Catholic fanatic who assassinated Henry IV of France in 1610.

entries.topic: Gaspar de Guzman, Count-Duke of Olivares
entries.answerline: Gaspar de Guzman, Count-Duke of Olivares
entries.note: Chief minister to Philip IV of Spain who tried and failed to reverse Spain's imperial decline.
Flemish cartographer whose 1569 map projection remains the standard for world maps.
1605 failed Catholic conspiracy led by Guy Fawkes to blow up England's Parliament and King James I.
Catholic conspirator caught guarding explosives in the failed 1605 Gunpowder Plot against Parliament.
French king killed in a jousting accident, whose death began decades of Wars of Religion under his sons.
Dutch jurist regarded as the father of international law, author of On the Law of War and Peace.
1653 written constitution establishing Oliver Cromwell as Lord Protector of England.
Catholic Tudor queen called "Bloody Mary" for burning Protestants while trying to reverse England's Reformation.
Dutch stadtholder and military reformer who professionalized the army during the Eighty Years' War.
1647 discussions among Cromwell's New Model Army over suffrage and England's future constitution.
Alliance of Protestant German princes formed to defend against Charles V's Catholic forces.
Radical German Reformation preacher who led peasants in the German Peasants' War, executed after defeat.
English royal house from Henry VII through Elizabeth I, spanning the Reformation and Renaissance.
Site of two 17th-18th-century wars between Swiss Catholic and Protestant cantons.
Archbishop of Canterbury whose High Church reforms under Charles I provoked Puritan and Scottish opposition.

sections.name: Absolutism and the Early Modern State
sections.blurb: Louis XIV's France, the rise of Russia, Prussia, and the Habsburg and Ottoman empires, the English constitutional struggles, the Dutch Golden Age, and the Enlightenment, roughly 1650-1789.

entries.note: Western European kingdom of the Capetians and Bourbons, dominant under Louis XIV, later transformed by revolution and Napoleon.
Scandinavian kingdom that was a Baltic great power under Gustavus Adolphus and Charles XII before turning to neutrality.
German-born Russian empress who expanded the empire, partitioned Poland, and patronized the Enlightenment after deposing her husband Peter III.
"Sun King" of France whose long reign at Versailles epitomized absolutist monarchy; fought the War of the Spanish Succession.
Baltic great power under Gustavus Adolphus and Charles XII, later reduced by defeat in the Great Northern War.
Russian tsar who westernized Russia, founded St. Petersburg, and defeated Sweden in the Great Northern War.
Habsburg capital that survived Ottoman sieges in 1529 and 1683 and hosted the 1815 congress redrawing post-Napoleonic Europe.
Scandinavian kingdom long allied with Norway, later losing Schleswig-Holstein to Prussia and Norway to Sweden.
England's capital since Roman times, devastated by the Great Fire of 1666; seat of Parliament and the monarchy.
Semi-autonomous warrior communities of the Russian and Ukrainian steppe frontier, led by figures like Bohdan Khmelnytsky.
1700-1721 war in which Peter the Great's Russia broke Swedish dominance of the Baltic under Charles XII.
1688 overthrow of James II by William III and Mary II, cementing Parliamentary supremacy in England.
Early-17th-century Russian succession crisis and foreign invasion following Tsar Feodor I's death, ended by the Romanovs' rise.

sections.name: Revolution and Napoleonic Europe
sections.blurb: The French Revolution, the Napoleonic Wars, and the Congress of Vienna's settlement, 1789-1815.

sections.name: Nationalism and Reform in Nineteenth-Century Europe
sections.blurb: Italian and German unification, the revolutions of 1848, Balkan nationalism and Ottoman decline, British and Irish political reform, and the Industrial Revolution, 1815-1914.
